title: One-Board Wooden Centerpiece Tray
summary: A long, narrow centerpiece tray for a rectangular or oval dining table, built entirely from one board.
description:
A round bowl centerpiece doesn't suit a long rectangular or oval table — it either looks lost in the middle or blocks sightlines across a narrow width. This tray solves that by being long and narrow on purpose, built entirely from a single board so its proportions naturally match a runner-style table rather than a round one.

Every piece — sides and bottom — comes from **one board**, which keeps the grain and color consistent across the whole tray instead of showing a seam between mismatched stock.

Cabinet pulls on each end turn it into something liftable and movable, rather than a fixed centerpiece you have to nudge around by its edges.

steps.title: Size and cut the tray pieces
steps.body: Measure your table first and pick a tray length that leaves clear space at each end rather than running edge to edge — this is a centerpiece, not a runner. Cut the bottom to that length at the board's full 11-1/4" width, then rip the side and end stock for the walls from the rest of the same 10 ft board so the grain and color stay consistent across the whole tray, which is the entire point of building it from one board. Decide now which pair of walls runs the full outside length: if the long sides run through, the two ends are cut to the bottom's 11-1/4" width and fit between them; if the ends run through, each end is 11-1/4" plus 3/4" of side stock at each corner and the sides shorten to match. Cut all four walls to the same height so the top edge sits level all the way round.

steps.title: Chamfer the edges (optional)
steps.body: If you want the softer edge, rout a chamfer along the top outside edge of each wall piece before assembly, while the parts are still flat and easy to clamp down — chamfering after glue-up means working around inside corners the bit cannot reach. Take it in two shallow passes rather than one deep one, and run a test cut on an offcut to set the depth. This is optional; a sanded, eased edge looks fine and needs no router.

steps.title: Glue up the tray
steps.body: Dry-fit all five pieces first and confirm the walls close square around the bottom before any glue goes on. Glue the walls to the bottom's edges and to each other at the corners, then clamp across the tray in both directions — with no nails or screws in this build the glue joint is the only thing holding it, so even clamping pressure and full glue coverage matter more than usual. Check the diagonals while it is still wet and nudge it square, then leave it clamped about an hour before handling.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the whole tray smooth inside and out, including the bottom, and ease every edge and corner — this piece gets slid around a dining table, so a sharp bottom edge will mark the table and a sharp top edge will catch. Break the inside corners by hand where the sander cannot reach. Then stain or paint, finishing the underside as well as the top so the board takes on moisture evenly and does not cup.

steps.title: Add the handles
steps.body: Center a cabinet pull on each end wall, marking both mounting-hole centers off the pull itself rather than measuring twice. Drill the through-holes from the outside face and back the exit side with scrap so the plywood-thin 3/4" wall does not blow out on the inside. Check that the pull's screws are short enough not to protrude into the tray, and swap for shorter ones if they are.
